coredata.dc:title: Selective determination of acetone by carbon nanodots based on inner filter effect
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

Wang, Xingyang
Chang, Quanhong
Shi, Wangzhou
Huang, Lei
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent sensing of acetone has been achieved taking advantage of the unique optical property of acetone. However, the complicated synthesis process of the fluorescent probes limited their application. Here, carbon nanodots (CNDs) derived from glucose were chosen as the sensing material for the first time, which could be obtained by a one-pot microwave-assisted synthesis within 2 min. The CND had ultraviolet excitation spectrum, resulting in an obvious overlap between its excitation band with the absorption band of acetone. The fluorescent quenching occurred via inner filter effect with fast response. The CNDs showed sensitive response to acetone with a detection limit of 0.09 vol%, which was comparable to most previously reported MOF probes. The results of our work indicated the feasibility of IFE-based sensing method, and demonstrated an efficient and convenient way for acetone detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Acetone detection
Fluorescent probe
Inner filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detail Investigation and Optimization of the IFE Process

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM (a), size distribution histogram (b) and HRTEM (c, d) images of the carbon nanodots prepared from glucose and ammonia.
(a) XRD pattern of the CNDs. (The black bars represent the peak position of the pattern). (b) The Raman spectrum of the CNDs.

(a) Typical UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence emission spectra of the CNDs in ethanol solution. The excitation wavelength was 260 nm. (b) FTIR spectrum of glucose and glucose-derived CNDs. (c) XPS survey spectrum of the CNDs. (d) The detailed (high-resolution) scans of C1s of the CNDs.

(a) Selectivity of CNDs for acetone over other organic interferences in ethanol. The intensity indicated the photoluminescence of the 446 nm peak of the CNDs (blank) or CNDs in the presense of different analytes. Λex = 260 nm. The volume percentage of all the analytes were 1%. (b) UV–vis spectra of acetone and other kind of analytes, and excitation spectrum of the CNDs.

(a) Fluorescence quenching of CNDs by various vol% of acetone. (b) Stern-Volmer plot of the fluorescent quenching of CNDs by acetone over the range of 0.1–1.3 vol%. The absorption value of the CNDs solution was set to 0.2.
Variation of the fluorescent intensity of the CNDs (I0) (a) and quenching efficiency of the CNDs by 1% acetone (I/I0) (b) on the concentration of the solution (represented by its absorption value).

head.ce:title: Selective determination of acetone by carbon nanodots based on inner filter effect

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Shanghai Normal University, Guilin Road 100, Shanghai 200234, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent sensing of acetone has been achieved taking advantage of the unique optical property of acetone. However, the complicated synthesis process of the fluorescent probes limited their application. Here, carbon nanodots (CNDs) derived from glucose were chosen as the sensing material for the first time, which could be obtained by a one-pot microwave-assisted synthesis within 2 min. The CND had ultraviolet excitation spectrum, resulting in an obvious overlap between its excitation band with the absorption band of acetone. The fluorescent quenching occurred via inner filter effect with fast response. The CNDs showed sensitive response to acetone with a detection limit of 0.09 vol%, which was comparable to most previously reported MOF probes. The results of our work indicated the feasibility of IFE-based sensing method, and demonstrated an efficient and convenient way for acetone detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Acetone is widely used as cosmetics solvent or denaturant these years, thus being found at trace level or even high concentration in the environment which results from industrial leaching or sewage disposal. Due to the high volatility of acetone and its potential toxic effects on the human organisms [1], there have been increased interest in acetone determination in water [2]. Moreover, detection of acetone in urine or blood can be used as a potential biomarker for diagnosis of diseases such as Diabetes Mellitus or starving [3,4].

The traditional analytical methods for detection of acetone were mostly based on chromatographic techniques [5,6]. Other kind of methods based on FTIR spectroscopy [7], ion mobility spectrometry [8] were also reported. These methods usually required complex equipment and long-time pre-treatment of the sample, and the response was quite slow.

Recently, fluorescent sensors, which mostly based on metal-organic frameworks (MOF) or coordination polymers (CPS), were also developed for the detection of acetone. The competition for the excitation energy between acetone and ligand of these complexes reduced the energy transfer from the ligand to the central metal ions, thus causing the fluorescence quenching [9–12]. Sensitive detection of acetone using this kind of materials has been widely reported. However, the synthesis method of these fluorescent probes was usually complicated, which limited their application.

In general, there have been various kind of methods reported in fluorescent sensing system. Beside the above method which based on competition of light absorption, energy transfer between a fluorescent donor and the tested acceptor has also been investigated widely. One of them is the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), which has been used for probing various biomolecules [13], toxins [14] or ion species [15]. However, the establishment of FRET-based sensors requires two fluorophores and a particular distance between the donor and the accepter, therefore always demands complex surface modification of the materials.

Another kind of method is called inner filter effect (IFE), which refers to the absorption of the emission or excitation light of fluorophores by absorbers. In recent years, IFE method has been widely used for detection of toxic mental ions [16] or micromolecules [17]. Compared with FRET process, IFE-based approach does not require subtle adjusting of the distance between fluorophore and absorber, making the design much simpler [18]. Besides, as the absorbance changes of the absorber are exponentially converted into the fluorescence changes of the fluorophore, an enhanced sensitivity is expected to be achieved with respect to observing the absorbance changes alone [19].

Quantum dots (QDs), a class of fluorescent nanomaterials, have been widely used as sensors [20,21]. Compared with the organic dye or MOF, QDs had advantages of high quantum yield, tunable emission band and stability against photobleaching. Particularly, carbon nanodots (CNDs), a new kind of nanomaterial developed in recent years, showed superiority of non-toxicity and easy synthetic methods [22] compared with the semiconductor QDs. Moreover, they were expected to be a better energy donor because all the emitters are located at the surface energy traps [23].

In this study, an IFE-based fluorescent sensing of acetone was first realized using carbon nanodots. The ultraviolet excitation spectra of the glucose-derived CNDs overlapped with the absorption band of acetone, making the IFE process possible. Furthermore, hydrogen bond could be easily formed between carbonyl group in acetone and hydroxyl groups on the surface of the CNDs, thus ensure the close distance between acetone molecules and CNDs to improve the sensitivity.
Glucose and ammonium hydroxide solution were purchased from Shanghai Chemical Reagents Company. Mili-Q water and ethanol were used as the solvent. All reagents were used without further purification.

Carbon nanodots were prepared via a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method according to the previous report [24]. Typically, 2 g glucose was dissolved in 10 ml deionized water. Then 1 ml ammonia was diluted to 7 ml, and added to the above glucose solution under stirring. The mixed solution was subjected to microwave irradiation for 2 min at 120 °C. The as-prepared CNDs were naturally cooled down to the room temperature and concentrated by rotary evaporation. Purification was done by dialysis (0.5 KD) for about 2 days. For detection of acetone, the obtained CNDs were freezing-dried and redispersed in ethanol.

For acetone detection, increasing amount of acetone was added to diluted CNDs solution in ethanol, and the change of the fluorescent intensity of the CNDs was measured after equilibrating for 2 min. To investigate the response of the CNDs to different analytes, acetone was changed with methanol, isopropanol, 1,2-ethanediol, hexane, cyclohexane, acetonitrile, toluene and chloroform. Typically, 1 ml ethanol solution of the above 3.0% organic solvents was freshly prepared, and mixed with 2 ml of diluted CNDs solution. The fluorescence quenching of the CNDs was measured in the case of different analytes.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were performed on a JEOL-2011 electron microscope (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200 KV. Samples for TEM and HRTEM were prepared by dropping CNDs solution onto a carbon-coated copper grid. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were measured on a Philips X'Pert diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Thermo-Nicolet transform infrared spectrometer (Nicolet/Nexus 670). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyse was carried out using an ESCALAB 250 spectrometer equipped with a monochromatic X-ray source with Al Ka excitation (1486.6 eV). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using a Lambda 950 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained using a Shimadzu RF-6310PC spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Columbia, MD, USA).

TEM and HRTEM images of glucose-derived CNDs are shown in Fig. 1 , The CNDs showed good monodispersed distribution with an average diameter of about 2.87 nm (Fig. 1a and Fig. 1b). The well-resolved lattice fringe in HRTEM images (Fig. 1c and d) confirmed the good crystalline of the CNDs. The lattice space of the CNDs was calculated to be 0.24 nm, which could be attributed to the (100) facet of graphitic carbon.

Fig. 2a shows the powder XRD pattern of the CNDs. The broad diffractive peak at 18.09° revealed the amorphous nature of the CNDs [25], while the peak at 26.31° (d002) was attributed to highly disordered carbon, similar to graphite lattice spacing [26,27]. In the Raman spectrum of the CNDs in Fig. 2b, the peak at 1587 cm−1 (G band) corresponded to the E2g mode of the graphite and was related to the vibration of sp2-bonded carbon atoms in a 2D hexagonal lattice [28]. The high fluorescence may disturbed the Raman characterization [27].

Typical UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the CNDs are shown in Fig. 3a. In the UV–vis spectrum, the peak centered at 278 nm could be assigned to π electron transition [28], which was commonly found in carbon nanodots. In the PL spectrum, with the excitation of the 260 nm laser, the CNDs showed visible emission centered at 446 nm resulted from the radiative recombination after interband transition [24].

The FTIR and XPS spectroscopy were employed to investigate the surface groups of the CNDs. Fig. 3b shows the FTIR spectra of glucose and glucose-derived CNDs. In both curves of glucose and CNDs, peaks at 3100–3500 cm−1 (OH) and 1080 cm−1 (CO) were found. Compared with the glucose source, a new absorption peak at 1617 cm−1 (aromatic CC) was found in the curve of CNDs, which indicated the formation of graphene structure [24].

XPS data of the CNDs shown in Fig. 3c indicated N doping in the nanoparticles, which resulted from the participation of ammonia in the formation of CNDs. The C1s spectrum in Fig. 3d revealed three types of carbon atoms: CC or CC at binding energy of 284.6 eV; COH at 286.1 eV; and COC 287.1 eV [27,29]. The surface components determined by XPS were in agreement with FTIR results.

In a general view of the formation process of the CNDs, the dehydration took place between –NH2 groups in ammonium hydroxide and OH groups in glucose, followed by aromatization and carbonization to form the nanodots [30]. As there were a plenty of hydroxyl groups in glucose, the resulted CNDs were expected to be covered with abundant hydroxyl groups on their surfaces. Thus, hydrogen bond could be formed between CO groups in acetone and OH groups on the surfaces of the CNDs [16,24]. This kind of interaction could ensure close distance between acetone and CNDs, thus facilitating the energy re-absorption by acetone, and resulted in high sensitivity and rapid response of the CNDs probe.

First, to evaluate the selectivity of the CNDs to acetone, a variety of organic solvents were tested. As shown in Fig. 4a, with the adding amount of 1.0 vol% acetone, the fluorescence of the CNDs solution was greatly quenched to about 25%. Meanwhile, other analytes had less effect on the fluorescence of CNDs, indicating their high selectivity to acetone.

This selectivity was achieved taking advantage of the unique optical property of acetone compared with other organic analytes. The UV–vis absorption spectra of acetone and other interferences are shown in Fig. 4b. As seen in the picture, the absorption band of acetone extended to 320 nm, which had largest overlap with the excitation spectrum of the CNDs (extended to about 240 nm), making the IFE process possible. This intrinsic optical property of acetone ensured the high selectivity of the sensing system, as confirmed by the experimental data in Fig. 4a.

To evaluate the sensitivity in detail, increased amounts of acetone were mixed with the CNDs solution, while the concentration of the CNDs in the mixed solutions was kept constant, and fluorescence response of these solutions were measured after 2 min of equilibration. As shown in Fig. 5a, a gradual decrease of the fluorescence intensity of the CNDs was observed upon addition of acetone. The relationship between the I/I0 and volume ratio of acetone is shown in Fig. 5b. With acetone amount at low concentration range, the fluorescent intensity of the CNDs decreased drastically. In this range, the relationship between I/I0 and volume percentage of acetone could be described by the Stern-Volmer Eq. (1) [16,31]:
(1)
I /
I 0
= 1 +
K s
C
where Ks is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, I and I0 represent the fluorescent intensity of the CNDs with and without acetone, respectively. A good linear correlation (R2 = 0.991) was found between the fluorescence response of CNDs and the acetone content within the range of 0.1–0.5 vol%. The detection limit (3σ/K) was determined to be 0.09 vol%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detail Investigation and Optimization of the IFE Process
ce:section.ce:para: To discuss the detail process of fluorescent quenching, and optimize the sensing condition, variation of the quenching effect of acetone was investigated by tuning the concentration of CNDs solution.

It is known that the accurate concentration of a quantum dot solution is difficult to be determined. As the absorption value of a dot solution is proportional to its concentration, here a serials of diluted CNDs ethanol solutions with a gradient concentration were prepared. The absorption value of them was measured and used to represent their concentration.

First, the variation of the original fluorescence intensity of the CNDs on the concentration of the solution (represented by its abs. value) was studied. In Fig. 6a, a linear response of the fluorescent intensity was found at the absorption value lower than 0.14, indicating a distance between the nanodot particles that could avoid interparticle energy transfer. At higher concentration, the increase of the fluorescent intensity gradually slowed down due to self-absorption, which is a well-known property of quantum dots [32].

Then 1% acetone was added to the above solutions, and fluorescence intensity of them was measured after 2 min. Fig. 6b shows the variation of quenching efficiency (I/I0) on the concentration of the solution. Here I and I0 represented the fluorescence intensity of the CNDs with and without acetone, respectively. As seen in the picture, the quenching effect of acetone was weak in a diluted CNDs solution (absorption value <0.2). This was due to the far distance between acetone molecule and nanoparticles, which could suppress the absorption of the emitted energy from CNDs by acetone. As the concentration of the solution increased, the nanoparticles became closer, and hydrogen bond could be formed between CO groups in acetone and OH groups on the surface of the CNDs, which further ensured the close distance between acetone molecules and CNDs. As a result, the quenching efficiency became higher, then tended to a saturation (abs >0.2). This kind of tendency was consistent with the proposed inner filter effect mechanism, which worked more efficiently in a concentrated solution.

We demonstrated a rapid, sensitive and selective detection of acetone in ethanol solution by fluorescent carbon nanodots. The easy formation of CNDs avoided complicated synthesis route in the case of organic dye or MOFs. Besides, the hydrogen bond between acetone and hydroxyl groups on the surface of CNDs could ensure a close distance between acetone molecules and the nanoparticles, thus resulted in a rapid response of the sensing system. The high quenching efficiency resulted from a relatively small spectral overlap demonstrated good feasibility and potential advantage of IFE-based sensing method. On the account of the presented results, this kind of approach is promising for applications in acetone sensing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (No. 18070502800) and the Natural Science Foundation of Shanghai (No. 16ZR1424400), Shanghai Municipal Education Committee Key Laboratory of Molecular Imaging Probes and Sensors for Shanghai Universities. Prof. Lei Huang appreciates the support of The Program for Professor of Special Appointment (Eastern Scholar) at Shanghai Institutions of Higher Learning.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: SID SInitial Assessment Report (SIAR) for the 9th SIAM: Acetone CAS No: 67-64-1, U. P., 1999, 1–118.
